title: Car Detailing Business
metaTitle: How to Start a Car Detailing Business — Real Costs, Earnings, and Steps
metaDescription: An honest guide to starting a mobile car detailing business: real startup costs, realistic earnings, ceramic coating upsells, time-per-car reality, and common mistakes.
bestFor: Detail-minded people who enjoy transforming a car's condition and want a mobile, low-overhead business with a clear premium upsell in coatings
biggestRisk: Damaging a customer's paint with improper machine polishing or buying jobs at low prices that don't reflect the real hours each car takes
whatItIs: A car detailing business deep-cleans and restores vehicles far beyond a car wash — interior shampooing and extraction, leather conditioning, exterior decontamination, paint correction (machine polishing out swirls and scratches), and protective coatings. The mobile model, where you bring water, power, and equipment to the customer's home or workplace, has become the dominant low-overhead path because it skips the cost of a fixed shop. The economics hinge on two things most beginners underestimate: how many real hours each car takes, and the premium upsell ladder. A basic wash-and-vacuum is low-margin, but paint correction and especially ceramic coatings command $500 to $2,000+ per car and are where serious detailers make their money.
dailyReality: A realistic day is one to three vehicles, and that surprises people: a thorough full detail takes 3 to 5 hours, a paint correction can run 6 to 10, and a multi-stage ceramic coating prep-and-apply spans one to two full days. You are physically working the entire time — vacuuming, scrubbing, clay-barring, running a dual-action polisher, applying products in a dust-controlled way — often crouched, reaching into footwells, and standing in heat. Mobile work adds setup and teardown of your water tank, generator, and gear at each stop, plus checking that the customer has space and you are not violating any HOA or local wastewater rules. Booking, quoting from photos, and managing weather cancellations fill the gaps.

startupCostsItemized.item: Pressure washer or foam cannon and wash supplies
Chemicals (APC, iron remover, clay, dressings, IPA, sealants)
Water tank, portable power/generator, and mobile setup
Ceramic coating kits and application supplies
Google Business Profile, booking page, branding

realEarnings.yearOne: Part-time beginners offering basic and full details typically earn $1,500 to $3,500 per month, limited by how few cars fit in a day and by early underpricing. A solo detailer going full-time with full details and some paint correction commonly reaches $3,500 to $6,000 per month once booked.
realEarnings.experienced: Experienced solo detailers who sell paint correction and ceramic coatings regularly often report $5,000 to $9,000 per month. Coatings are the lever — a single ceramic job at $800 to $2,000 can equal several basic details, so the mix of premium work, not the number of cars, drives earnings here.
realEarnings.topEarners: Top detailers and small shops with a coating-heavy book, a strong reputation, and one or two trained techs gross $15,000 to $50,000+ per month, often from a fixed studio that lets them work weather-independently and command higher coating prices. Reaching this requires real skill certification, a portfolio, premium positioning, and either a shop lease or multiple mobile rigs.
realEarnings.whatAffectsMost: Service mix matters most. A detailer doing only $150 basic washes is capped by hours, while one selling $800 to $2,000 ceramic coatings earns multiples on the same day. After that, realistic time-per-car pricing and skill at paint correction without causing damage separate profitable detailers from busy, broke ones.
realEarnings.perHour: Basic and full details net roughly $30 to $60 per hour of actual work solo. Paint correction and ceramic coatings push effective rates to $60 to $120+ per hour because the price reflects skill, not just time. Counting setup, teardown, driving, and quoting, realistic mobile blended rates often land at $30 to $70 per hour.

howToStart: Week 1: Buy a dual-action polisher, a wet/dry vac and extractor, a foam cannon, and core chemicals. Practice machine polishing on a junkyard panel or your own car until you can correct paint without burning through clear coat. Get liability insurance with care-custody-control coverage before touching a customer's car.

Week 2: Set tiered pricing (basic, full detail, paint correction, ceramic) based on real timed jobs, not competitors' lowball rates. Build a simple booking page and Google Business Profile, and offer your first cars a portfolio rate in exchange for detailed before/after photos.

Month 1: Complete your first paid details and photograph every result in good light — your portfolio is your single best sales tool. Confirm your mobile setup handles water and power at customer sites, and learn the local rules on wastewater runoff.

Days 30-90: Introduce the upsell ladder, offering paint correction and ceramic coatings to every full-detail client, since coatings are where the real money is. Track true time per service so you stop undercharging, and start collecting Google reviews aggressively.

Months 3-6: Get a recognized ceramic coating certification to justify premium pricing and accreditation-backed warranties, decide whether a fixed studio would let you do weather-independent coating work, and consider a helper for the labor-heavy prep steps.

mustHave: Genuine patience and attention to detail — customers inspect their cars closely
Steady, careful hands and the discipline to learn machine polishing before charging for it
Comfort quoting and selling premium services like correction and coatings
canLearn: Safe dual-action paint correction without burning or hazing the clear coat
Interior extraction, odor removal, leather care, and stain treatment
Ceramic coating prep and application in a dust-controlled, contaminant-free way
separators: Selling and applying ceramic coatings, which command multiples of basic detail prices
Pricing each service to the real hours it takes instead of competing with cheap washes
A polished before/after portfolio and certification that justify premium positioning

whatPeopleGetWrong: Drastically underestimating time per car, then pricing a 4-hour full detail like a 1-hour wash
Competing on price against drive-through car washes instead of selling correction and coatings
Attempting paint correction or coatings before they are skilled, then causing burns, holograms, or coating failures
Skipping care-custody-control insurance, leaving them exposed when they damage a customer's vehicle
Ignoring local wastewater and HOA rules for mobile work, risking fines for runoff into storm drains
Treating every car as a one-off and never building the coating-and-maintenance upsell that drives real income

toolsAndEquipment.item: Dual-action polisher with pads and compounds
toolsAndEquipment.note: Enables paint correction, the gateway to high-margin work. Learn it on scrap panels first to avoid burning paint.
Core interior tools. An extractor lifts embedded dirt a vacuum cannot and elevates your full-detail results.
For safe contact-light washing that avoids adding swirl marks before polishing.
Cleans vents, seams, and upholstery and helps with sanitizing and odor jobs.
The premium upsell. Manufacturer certification (e.g. Gtechniq, CarPro, Ceramic Pro) lets you offer warranties and charge more.

toolsAndEquipment.item: Mobile rig: water tank, generator, and storage
toolsAndEquipment.note: Lets you serve customers at home or work without their utilities. Add once demand justifies it.

methods: A strong before/after photo portfolio on Instagram and a Google Business Profile — visual proof sells detailing better than words
Google Business Profile with reviews for people searching for mobile detailing locally
Local Facebook, Nextdoor, and car-enthusiast and truck groups where owners value clean vehicles
Asking satisfied clients for reviews and referrals, especially after a dramatic transformation
Partnering with used-car dealers, fleet operators, and realtors for recurring volume work
howToFindCustomers.whereCustomersAre: Car enthusiasts, busy professionals, and luxury and truck owners who value their vehicles and will pay for correction and coatings, plus dealers and fleets needing volume. Coating buyers tend to be owners of newer or higher-value cars.
howToFindCustomers.timeToClientBase: Most detailers land their first jobs within one to three weeks once they have a few portfolio photos. Building a steady book with regular coating work usually takes three to six months of consistent results, reviews, and a growing portfolio.
howToFindCustomers.wasteOfTime: Paid ads and heavy branding before you have a portfolio. Detailing is sold on visible transformation, so before/after photos and reviews convert far better than advertising spend early on.

howItScales.toFullTime: Yes, but the path runs through premium services, not volume. A solo mobile detailer reaches full-time income faster by selling paint correction and ceramic coatings than by doing more basic washes, because hours-per-car caps how many vehicles fit in a day.
howItScales.hireAndStepBack: Possible but skill-dependent. Coatings and correction demand trained hands, so hiring means thoroughly training techs and risking quality and warranty issues if they cut corners. Many owners instead scale by raising prices and moving upmarket rather than adding labor; stepping back requires documented processes and trusted, skilled techs.
howItScales.sellable: A detailing business with a fixed studio, recurring fleet or dealer contracts, certifications, and a brand is sellable. A purely owner-skill-based mobile operation is harder to transfer because customers buy the owner's hands and reputation, not a system.
howItScales.whatScalingRequires: Moving into high-margin coatings, manufacturer certifications and warranties, a portfolio and premium positioning, possibly a fixed studio for weather-independent coating work, and either trained techs or multiple rigs. Pricing to real time-per-car is the foundation everything else builds on.

strongFit: You are meticulous and genuinely enjoy transforming a car's condition
You are willing to master machine polishing and coatings before charging premium prices
You want a mobile, low-overhead start with a clear path to high-ticket work
You are comfortable selling upgrades and quoting from real time-per-car math
poorFit: You want fast, high-volume income from many quick cars a day
You are impatient with detailed, repetitive, physically demanding work
You are unwilling to invest the practice time to polish paint safely
You will not carry insurance covering damage to vehicles in your care
askYourself: Am I willing to spend weeks practicing paint correction before I charge for it?
Will I price each service to the real hours it takes, or get trapped competing with cheap washes?
Can I learn to sell coatings and corrections, since that is where the real money is?

faqs.question: Do I need a license to start a car detailing business?
faqs.answer: Most areas require only a general business registration, but mobile detailing has a specific wrinkle: many municipalities regulate wastewater runoff, and washing cars where soapy water enters storm drains can bring fines. You also need general liability insurance with care-custody-control coverage, which protects you when you damage a vehicle in your care. Check local environmental and HOA rules before detailing on the street.

faqs.question: How long does it really take to detail a car?
faqs.answer: More than beginners expect. A thorough full detail takes 3 to 5 hours, a single-stage paint correction 6 to 10, and a multi-stage ceramic coating job can span one to two days including decontamination, correction, and curing. This time reality is why pricing a full detail like a quick car wash is the most common way detailers end up overworked and underpaid.

faqs.question: What is paint correction and why does it matter?
faqs.answer: Paint correction is machine-polishing the clear coat to remove swirl marks, scratches, and oxidation, restoring gloss. It is the skill that unlocks high-margin work, because a properly corrected car is what a ceramic coating should be applied over. It is also where you can do real damage — burning through clear coat is a costly, irreversible mistake — so practice on scrap panels before charging customers.

faqs.question: How much can I charge for a ceramic coating?
faqs.answer: Ceramic coatings typically run $500 to $2,000+ depending on the vehicle, the coating's durability rating, and how much correction the paint needs first. They are the single biggest profit lever in detailing because one coating can equal several basic details in revenue. Manufacturer certification lets you offer backed warranties and command the higher end of that range.

faqs.question: Mobile or a fixed shop — which is better to start?
faqs.answer: Mobile is the lower-overhead way to start since you skip rent, and most beginners begin there. The tradeoff is weather, setup and teardown time, and harder dust control for coatings. A fixed studio costs more but enables weather-independent work, cleaner coating conditions, and higher pricing, so many detailers start mobile and move to a studio once coating demand justifies the rent.

faqs.question: Do I need experience to start detailing?
faqs.answer: Basic washing and interior cleaning can be learned quickly, but the profitable services — paint correction and ceramic coatings — require real skill you must build before charging for it, which is why this sits above pure-beginner trades. Plan to practice machine polishing on your own and scrap panels, and consider a manufacturer coating certification before offering those services.

faqs.question: How quickly can I start making money?
faqs.answer: Most detailers complete their first paid jobs within one to three weeks of buying equipment and posting a few before/after photos. Reaching a steady book with regular high-margin coating work usually takes three to six months of consistent results, a growing portfolio, and steady reviews.

dataSources: U.S. Bureau of Labor Statistics — Automotive and Personal Care Services occupational data
IBISWorld — Car Wash and Auto Detailing industry overview (US market size and trends)
Detailing product and coating manufacturers (CarPro, Gtechniq, Ceramic Pro) for service pricing and certification norms
Operator communities (r/AutoDetailing, Detailing World, AutoGeek forums) for time-per-car and real-world earnings
